*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In Arkansas, most people who are incarcerated become eligible for parole release—called “transfer to community correction”—after serving minimum terms of one-third or one-half of their judicial maximum sentences, which may be reduced by “meritorious good time” credits. Sorting into the one-third and one-half categories is based on the seriousness determination made by the Sentencing Commission. Substantial changes in release law will take effect in 2025 under Act 659 (2023).¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2019, 6 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 32 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 5 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 49 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 94 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 34 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 43 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 57 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for aggravated or simple assault (31%) and property (30%) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2019, 32 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology ATTENTION, ERROR: This state should not be in the tool

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the percentage of the max. sentence (33 percent for most people, and 70 percent for those with an offense type that might have special eligibility restrictions). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Because of this, data reported for Arkansas is from 2010, except for the 2023 estimate.

Data is excluded for years where more than 10 percent of the relevant cases are missing the admission year or the max. sentence length variables, and for years when more than 40 percent of the relevant cases areare missing PEY. Relevant cases are all except those with life sentences, sentences of less than a year, and admission types that are not new court commitments or not available. All figures are calculated among relevant cases only.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Arkansas *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, February 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-03/arkansas_doi_report_2_13_23](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-03/arkansas_doi_report_2_13_23).pdf

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43. For offenses with special restrictions, see Kevin R. Reitz, Allegra Lukac, and Edward E. Rhine, Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size State Report: Arkansas (Minneapolis, MN: Robina Institute, 2023), 10, 33..

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.Arkansas Department of Corrections, Division of Correction Board Report January 2024 (Pine Bluff, AR: Arkansas Department of Corrections, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://doc.arkansas.gov/wp-content/uploads/Division-of-Correction-Directors-Board-Report_-January-2024.pdf, 1. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 5 percent, changing from 16,204 in 2010 to 17,022 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 54 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2020, 92 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2020, 30 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2020, 21 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for drug offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2020, people released from prison increased 95 percent, dropping from 15,198 in 2010 to 29,674 in 2020. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In 2020, 56 percent of parole-eligible people released in Arkansas were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 6 percent from 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 41 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 68 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 83 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 36 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 41 percent of people released from prison were incarcerated for drug offenses. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Parole Release ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_black_Arkansas.png){width=1180} ```{=html} In 2019, Black people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.5 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Arkansas. ``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_Arkansas.png){width=1156} ```{=html} In 2019, Hispanic people were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 2 times higher than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Arkansas.
Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity. ``` ### Sex ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_Arkansas.png){width=1190} ```{=html} In 2019, males were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 2.4 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in Arkansas.

## Notes on Disparities Interpret small numbers with caution, as they may introduce variability and lead to less reliable estimates. We do not report on people of "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" because the numbers are generally smaller, and disparities analyses require sufficient sample sizes to produce meaningful comparisons. In the NCRP data, people of "Other race(s)" may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Due to the small numbers in these groups, reporting could lead to unreliable or misleading results. The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than one means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than one means they experience it at a lower rate. State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people. ::: ::: {.cell} :::
